focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Ed's crooked little crown floated over Dry Well on the nav glass, yellow grease pencil trembling every time Jet corrected the yoke. He flew the Bebop lower than he needed to, low enough that the ship's shadow kept finding the broken ground and sliding over it like spilled oil. South of Tharsis the settlements thinned fast. Towers got shorter. Roads stopped pretending to go somewhere. Old terraforming hardware sat out in the open where everyone could see how much money had died there. Mirror fields lay in cracked silver strips across the flats. Pump stations leaned into the dust with their intake mouths packed solid. A canal cut straight through the plain for thirty kilometers and then simply ended, as if the planet had gotten tired of being argued with.

The cockpit glass held the whole wrong-colored afternoon. Mars wasn't red from up close. It was rust, brick, old blood, oxidized machinery, dust blown thin over black stone. The light turned everything into some variation of worn metal. Jet kept one hand on the controls and one hand near the trim, feeling the age of the ship through the small corrections it wanted from him. The Bebop had a loose sympathy in the yoke on long flights. It never flew exactly straight unless you kept reminding it.

One bare foot hooked over the arm of the chair. The toes flexed in time with a tune only Ed could hear. The goggles sat up on Ed's forehead, held there by habit and static, and a measuring spoon from Jet's galley had somehow ended up tucked through the hair like an ornament.

"Dead greenhouse at two o'clock," Ed said. "No, correction, dead greenhouse cluster. Big crunchy salad bowl. Nobody watered the tomato kingdom."

Jet glanced left. Ribbed domes hunched out of the flats, half collapsed, their translucent skins to...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...on the pavement, but one corner of her mouth moved before it went flat again. Their steps hit the dust in the same beat for the rest of the block.

Jet shifted the groceries in his hand. One of the cheap bags gave a small plastic complaint. He looked down. The corner of the rice package had started sawing through the seam.

He should have doubled it at the stall. He had been doing the numbers in his head instead.

The Bebop's paid berth came into view between two cargo fences, old hull paint dulled by dust, one docking light flickering in a way that had definitely moved from tomorrow's problem to tonight's. Jet watched it blink and felt the day lining itself up into the next day already: unload, cook, patch the light, see what else broke while they were gone, decide whether fifteen hundred woolongs counted as hope or insult.

He heard himself speak before he had decided to.

"New rule," he said. "Next poster number means nothing till Ed checks the live docket and we count the split at the table before the ship takes a bite. Nobody disappears before the count."

Faye shifted the vegetables higher on her hip. "Good. My money gets to be money before it turns into one of your valves."

Jet glanced at her. "Fine. Ship rule."

"Same time tomorrow, or are we still not talking?"

Spike made a small sound that could have been a laugh or a cough dragged backward through his nose. He did not answer.

Faye didn't either.

She stepped closer, took the splitting bag out of Jet's right hand, and twisted the plastic around the rice until the seam stopped whining. The handle left a red line across her palm.

Jet let go.

The bags whispered against their coats after that. They kept walking toward the ship with the groceries between them and cumin still caught in the cuffs of their coats.
